title_guess: Chedworth Roman Villa (short trip, Peter's birthday)

pages.text:
police, a very nice policeman. All announcements start in Welsh & change to English so it look for-ever. The [?ex] policeman couldn't work out where I was. We didn't have the same pronounciation for Llanmador & I forgot the word Gower. He finally decided that I wasn't in St. Davids & kindly put me through to another police station but they never answered the phone. Then a man walked past & said he would get his Wellingtons & sort it out. Later we saw them trotting along the beach to join other horses. They all have lovely long hair. In the too very high tides horses & cows are moved off the beach but the sheep are left standing in the water.

__Monday__
We have driven quite a tortuous route over the Heads of the Valleys – new roads being built, & stopped for lunch at Raglan Castle.

__Tuesday May 25th__ Peter's Birthday 11.10. We left for a short trip to see Chadworth Roman Villa While eating lunch we heard that Kaz & John are expecting their second baby.

[margin, vertical] There are also 2 buckeye trees from US, grafted onto English horse chestnuts.

[margin, vertical, lower] Chadworth Roman Villi [illegible lines]

captions: Chadworth Roman Villa / Mosaic floor of the Warm Room (Tepidarium) in the hot living / C4
pages.text:
[printed postcard/information card — Chedworth Roman Villa]

Chedworth Roman Villa was discovered in 1864 when a gamekeeper, out ferreting for rabbits, found small stone cubes – loose tesserae – from one of the buried mosaics. The site was swiftly due to reveal extensive walls, bath houses and fine mosaics from one of the most significant Roman villas in Britain.

The 19th century landowner, Lord Eldon, came to survey the finds, took shelter buildings over the most delicate ruins, and had a small museum for the artefacts. The estate was sold in the 1920s and the Villa was bought for the nation through public subscription.

Chedworth Roman Villa was at its greatest in the fourth century, as a large and luxurious country house, with many domestic comforts such as underfloor heating and two bath houses.

[handwritten, left margin, vertical]
Chadworth Roman Villa
Mosaic floor of the Warm Room
(Tepidarium) in the hot living
C4

[handwritten, left margin, vertical, continuation from L page]
There are also 2 buckeye trees from US,
grafted onto English horse chestnuts
(This is the buckeye state)
The Romans brought all seeds – still here!

[photo: interior photo of mosaic floor and hypocaust pillars at Chedworth Roman Villa]
